title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: AI and the Future of the Justice System

texts.A: The systematic integration of artificial intelligence tools into judicial sentencing processes represents a logical and long-overdue step toward achieving the fundamental legal ideal of equal and consistent treatment for all defendants, regardless of which judge they happen to appear before. Judges, being fallible human beings, are demonstrably subject to the effects of fatigue, accumulated personal prejudices, unconscious implicit biases, and what psychologists term 'decision fatigue' or 'analysis paralysis.' [37]A well-trained algorithm, systematically exposed to vast datasets of legal precedents and consistently applied sentencing guidelines, has the potential to offer a level of consistency and predictability that no traditional courtroom has ever been able to reliably achieve.[/37] The goal is not to eliminate the judge but to provide a transparent 'mathematical anchor' for fairness. [39]While many understandably fear the mysterious 'black box' of AI decision-making, I believe the far more dangerous and consequential 'black box' has always been the opaque and unauditable human mind itself.[/39]
texts.B: The seductive idea that computer algorithms are inherently 'neutral,' 'objective,' and therefore fundamentally more fair than human judgment is a genuinely dangerous fallacy that is gaining alarming institutional acceptance with very little critical scrutiny. [37]AI sentencing models are necessarily trained on historical legal data, which means they systematically codify, replicate, and actively amplify the existing structural and systemic inequalities embedded within decades of biased criminal justice practice.[/37] If the historical record was biased—and the evidence overwhelmingly confirms it was—then the AI derived from that record will be biased too, but now cloaked in a spurious and intimidating veneer of mathematical objectivity and scientific authority that makes it far harder for defence lawyers and defendants to effectively challenge. We are, in plain terms, automating injustice at scale. [40]A judge's fundamental and irreplaceable role is not merely to mechanically apply a predetermined formula, but to genuinely understand the unique human context of each case and to make space for the possibility of rehabilitation and redemption—qualities a machine lacks by definition.[/40]
texts.C: [39]I share the view that human judges are demonstrably fallible and that the systemic inconsistencies in sentencing represent a genuine injustice that demands urgent reform, but the appropriate and proportionate solution is emphatically not to hand over the judicial gavel to a profit-driven technology corporation operating behind closed doors.[/39] [38]The overwhelming majority of currently deployed legal AI systems are entirely proprietary and commercially confidential, meaning that the general public, defendants, and their lawyers fundamentally cannot examine, challenge, or understand the precise mechanisms by which life-altering decisions are being calculated and justified.[/38] I believe AI could genuinely serve as a useful and legitimate tool for more routine administrative judicial tasks—systematically organizing complex evidence or automatically checking for procedural errors—but it should never be deployed to determine a person's liberty. [40]However, I must concede that I disagree with the premise that 'human context' is always or automatically a benefit; often it is precisely where the most harmful and persistent biases most insidiously enter the system.[/40]
texts.D: The prevailing public and academic debate over artificial intelligence in law is excessively and unhelpfully focused on the dramatic question of automated 'sentencing' while almost entirely overlooking what may prove to be a far more significant and democratizing application: radically improving and equalizing access to basic legal advice and representation. The real and most impactful revolution that AI promises will be in providing genuinely affordable, high-quality legal guidance to the millions of people who currently cannot afford professional legal counsel and therefore navigate the justice system at a profound and systemic disadvantage. AI has the genuine potential to meaningfully democratize access to justice by helping ordinary citizens navigate bewildering legal bureaucracies. [38]While I share the legitimate skepticism regarding the current opacity and accountability issues with these systems, I firmly believe that with strict and enforceable open-source transparency requirements, we can develop AI tools that are ultimately more consistently accountable than any human committee or judicial panel has historically proven to be.[/38]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether AI increases fairness in sentencing?
questions.explanation: Expert A argues AI offers a 'mathematical anchor' for fairness and eliminates bias. Expert B argues AI 'codifies and amplifies' existing inequalities, effectively 'automating injustice.'

questions.question: Which expert shares C's concern about the lack of transparency in current legal AI systems?
questions.explanation: Expert C mentions that most legal AI is 'proprietary' and hidden. Expert D also 'shares the skepticism regarding the transparency of these systems' and calls for open-source requirements.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to A regarding the inherent flaws of human judgment?
questions.explanation: Expert A speaks of the 'black box' of the human mind and personal prejudices. Expert C also 'shares the view that human judges are fallible' and that human context is where 'harmful biases creep in.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on whether the unique 'human context' is an essential part of justice?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes a judge must 'understand the unique human context' for justice. Expert C disagrees, stating that 'human context' is often where harmful biases enter the system.
